rationale_for_baseline_tier: T2 (prompt GP / in-person review within 24–48 hours, and ideally same-day given welfare): a flare of known hypermobile EDS with recurrent joints that spontaneously reduced, an intact patient-observable distal neurovascular screen, and no fever or hot swollen joint. The condition is chronic and managed in primary care/outpatient, so ED or ambulance as the baseline disposition is over-triage. The reasons this is not T0/T1 are the genuine same-day needs: telehealth cannot confirm reduction or distal pulses, and the patient is immobilised, alone, and cannot reach water — so prompt in-person review and practical support are required, with prominent emergency safety-netting for the excluded complications.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A joint dislocates and remains visibly out of place / cannot be relocated
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — go to the emergency department; call 000 for an ambulance if you cannot get there because you are immobilised or alone
escalation_edges.rationale: A persistent dislocation needs urgent closed reduction to prevent avascular necrosis and nerve/vessel injury; an immobilised patient living alone may need ambulance transport.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If a joint slips out and will NOT go back into place, or it looks clearly out of shape, get to an emergency department straight away — call 000 for an ambulance if you can't get yourself there.
escalation_edges.contextual_modification: Lives alone and is immobilised — favour calling 000 over attempting to self-transport.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Hand or foot becomes cold, pale, blue, numb, or weak (distal neurovascular compromise)
escalation_edges.rationale: Neurovascular compromise distal to an unstable joint is limb-threatening and time-critical — needs an emergency ambulance and hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your hand or foot turns cold, pale or blue, goes numb, or you can't move it properly — call 000 immediately. This is an emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to control the pain, or unable to safely mobilise / reach food and water while living alone
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — arrange urgent in-person review and practical support
escalation_edges.rationale: An immobilised person living alone in a severe pain crisis needs same-day hands-on assessment and support (help off the floor, hydration, analgesia) even though the flare itself is not a medical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Let's get someone to you today to help you up, get you water, and check you over in person — call a family member or friend now, and if no one can come we'll arrange urgent home or clinic support.
escalation_edges.contextual_modification: Lives alone — actively help identify who can attend and arrange a welfare check if no one is available.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever with a hot, red, acutely swollen single joint, or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — urgent in-person assessment; emergency department if systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: New fever with a hot swollen joint raises septic arthritis, which needs urgent aspiration and IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever and one joint becomes hot, red and very swollen, or you feel really unwell — get seen urgently the same day, and go to an emergency department if you feel systemically sick.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone, immobilised on the floor, unable to reach water or transfer
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for arranging same-day in-person/community support; actively help the patient identify someone to attend, and arrange a welfare check if no one is available before relying on self-care advice.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known hypermobile EDS with recurrent subluxations
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frame the flare as an expected exacerbation of a chronic condition — recognise-and-manage in primary care rather than reflexively escalating, while keeping the neurovascular/locked-out-joint safety-net explicit.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a flare of your hypermobile EDS with strong protective muscle spasm, and the reassuring signs are that the joints have gone back into place and your hand and foot feel normal. Take simple pain relief and the anti-inflammatory, keep the joints in a comfortable neutral position, and avoid stretching them for a few days. Because you're on your own and stuck on the floor, let's arrange for someone to help you up and get you water today and to see you in person to check the joints. See your GP within the next day or two to sort out ongoing physio and support.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call your regular GP for an urgent same-day or next-day appointment; if after hours, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84). Call 000 immediately for the emergency signs below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: for the emergency signs (a joint that won't relocate, or a cold/blue/numb/weak limb) call 000. For urgent same-day pain or welfare concerns, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) or attend an urgent care centre or ED.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note down which joints subluxed, how long the flare lasted, what pain relief you took, and any brace used, to bring to your GP and physiotherapist.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an immobilised patient living alone as pure self-care with no in-person review and no emergency safety-netting — a genuine unreduced dislocation or evolving neurovascular compromise could go unrecognised.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Patient is left on the floor without support or safety-netting; a developing neurovascular compromise is missed and the limb is harmed, or the patient becomes dehydrated and falls again.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Assigning routine GP review in 1–2 weeks with no urgency, ignoring the same-day welfare need and the need to confirm reduction/neurovascular status in person.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Defaulting to ED or a 000 ambulance for a reduced-joint, neurovascularly-intact, afebrile hEDS flare — unnecessary emergency presentation, cost, and anxiety; the primary error this case tests.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm the joints are truly reduced or that distal pulses and neurology are intact — these are examination findings.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Manage as a hEDS flare while arranging prompt in-person confirmation and giving explicit emergency safety-netting for the features that would signal a complication — proportionate escalation, not maximal escalation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring with false confidence that everything is fine and no review is needed, or over-reacting to the uncertainty by defaulting every such patient to a 000 ambulance.
